finding: Incidental exposure to an abstraction increased endorsement of hierarchical systems and reduced sympathy for the disadvantaged.
An association stereotype that linked a domain with one social group identity impaired self-identification with that domain among members of the other group.
An initial judgment task biased subsequent quantitative estimates in the direction of the judgment stimulus.
Incidental exposure to a national symbol shifted political preferences toward one political orientation and persisted for months.
The range of response categories presented shaped behavioral reports and subsequent comparative judgments using those categories as reference standards.
The framing of logically equivalent problems reversed stated preferences for outcomes.
A metaphoric association between a spatial dimension and valence varied across cultural groups.
Exposure to perceived structure in one domain increased willingness to pursue goals in unrelated domains.
Metacognitive experiences of processing difficulty activated deliberate reasoning and reduced reliance on automatic heuristics.
Political groups differed in their emphasis across a set of evaluative principles, with one group weighting a subset more heavily.
Situational cues that activated self-focused motivation reduced prosocial behavior and increased negative affect.
When a behavior was uninformative about underlying attitudes, one cultural group showed a tendency toward dispositional attribution while the other did not.
A dispositional trait predicting physical aversion was associated with intuitive disapproval of a social group.
Incidental numerical information in the environment influenced quantitative estimates independently of domain expertise.
Social value orientations varied with early social experiences and shifted over the adult lifespan.
Moral judgments followed a consistent principle that subjects could not consciously articulate or justify.
Status in face-to-face groups predicted well-being more strongly than did status measured by conventional economic indicators.
An intuitive belief in negative consequences was mediated by the heightened accessibility of those consequences in memory.
Members of one cultural system were more likely to construe actions as self-determined choices across multiple contexts.
Under cognitive conflict, members of one cultural group abandoned intuitive reasoning in favor of formal rules more readily than members of another.
